title: Death rate from road injuries
subtitle: The annual number of deaths from road injuries per 100,000 people. Deaths include those from drivers and passengers, motorcyclists, cyclists and pedestrians.
caption:
Death rate from road injuries

The annual number of deaths from road injuries per 100,000 people. Deaths include those from drivers and passengers, motorcyclists, cyclists and pedestrians.

This choropleth map displays the death rate from road injuries per 100,000 people for the year 2021, using a color scale ranging from light beige for rates near zero to dark red for rates approaching 70. Greenland is marked with diagonal hatching, indicating no data is available for that region. The lowest values, represented by the lightest beige shade corresponding to the 0–10 band, cover the majority of North America, Western Europe, Australia, and parts of South America such as Chile and Argentina. Moving into the 10–20 and 20–30 bands, the map shows a prevalence of medium-light orange shades across much of Eastern Europe, Russia, and large portions of South America including Brazil and Colombia. Higher rates are concentrated in specific regions; North Africa features Algeria in the 30–40 band, while a cluster of countries in West Africa, including Mali and Guinea-Bissau, along with nations in Southern Africa like Botswana and Mozambique, fall within the 40–50 and 50–60 ranges. The highest visible mortality rates appear in isolated pockets, with South Sudan and the Democratic Republic of the Congo shaded in the darkest red tones, placing them in the upper bands between 60 and 70 deaths per 100,000 people. Similarly, a distinct dark red area is visible in Southeast Asia, specifically covering Cambodia, indicating a rate within the highest legend categories. The distribution reveals a gradient where high-income regions generally occupy the lower bands, while specific areas in Africa and Southeast Asia occupy the highest bands on the provided scale.
